news_summary: Groundbreaking space exploration advances with the launch of the most detailed universe survey, new NASA moon base science initiatives, and recognition of outstanding researchers driving innovation.
news_summary_fr: L'exploration spatiale connaît des avancées révolutionnaires grâce au lancement du relevé le plus détaillé de l'univers, aux nouvelles initiatives scientifiques de la NASA concernant la base lunaire et à la mise à l'honneur de chercheurs exceptionnels, moteurs de l'innovation.
news_summary_es: La exploración espacial avanza de forma revolucionaria con el lanzamiento del estudio más detallado del universo, las nuevas iniciativas científicas de la NASA para la base lunar y el reconocimiento a investigadores destacados que impulsan la innovación.

articles.title: The most detailed survey of the universe ever conducted starts now
articles.summary: The Vera C. Rubin Observatory in Chile is beginning its extraordinary survey of the southern sky, which will use the largest camera ever built to map the solar system, the galaxy and beyond
articles.content:
The Vera C. Rubin Observatory in Chile is beginning its extraordinary survey of the southern sky, which will use the largest camera ever built to map the solar system, the galaxy and beyond

A field of stars in the constellation Lupus captured by the Vera C. Rubin Observatory NSF–DOE Vera C. Rubin Observatory/NOIRLab/SLAC​/AURA

The Vera C. Rubin Observatory in Chile is finally beginning its mammoth survey of the universe. After a year of testing and calibration, it is starting the Legacy Survey of Space and Time, which is poised to become the most detailed record of the universe ever captured.

“Today, we begin filming the greatest cosmic movie ever made,” said Brian Stone at the US National Science Foundation in a statement.

Advertisement

For the next decade, Rubin will collect about 10 terabytes of data every night in the form of hundreds of high-resolution images of the southern sky. Each image will cover an area about 40 times the size of the full moon, and the completed survey will include nearly the entirety of the sky that is visible from the southern hemisphere.

This treasure trove of data will serve several purposes. The first, which has already begun, is to alert researchers to anything changing in the night sky, such as the appearance of supernovae or the motion of asteroids and comets.

“Millions of alerts in just the last couple of months show that Rubin is up and running as a discovery machine,” said Phil Marshall at Stanford University in California, who is part of the Rubin team. “Now we’re putting it all together.”

Free newsletter Sign up to Launchpad Bring the galaxy to your inbox every month, with the latest space news, launches and astronomical occurrences from New Scientist’s Leah Crane. Sign up to newsletter

These alerts have already led to the discovery of more than 11,000 new asteroids, and they are expected to result in the most complete inventory of solar system objects ever created.

In addition to canvassing the solar system, Rubin will provide information about more distant objects, building a detailed map of the Milky Way galaxy and peering deeper into the universe.

An early-release image (above) shows a sea of stars, interstellar gas and even distant galaxies. Such deep, detailed images taken again and again over 10 years will enable researchers to study rare cosmic events and even gain insight into dark matter, dark energy and the expansion of the universe.
articles.reasoning: The article reports the start of the Vera C. Rubin Observatory's Legacy Survey of Space and Time, a decade-long, large-scale astronomical survey using the largest camera ever built. This survey will generate an unprecedented detailed map of the southern sky, discovering thousands of new asteroids and providing valuable data for understanding cosmic phenomena, dark matter, and dark energy. The impact is broad and significant for the scientific community and humanity's understanding of the universe.
articles.personality_title: Vera C. Rubin Observatory begins decade-long survey to map the universe
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – The Vera C. Rubin Observatory in Chile is a new telescope facility designed to study the southern sky. It features the largest camera ever built for astronomy and aims to observe the universe in great detail.

**What happened** – After a year of testing, the observatory has started its Legacy Survey of Space and Time. Over the next ten years, it will take hundreds of high-resolution images every night, covering nearly the entire southern sky visible from Earth.

**Impact** – This survey will create the most detailed map of the solar system, our galaxy, and distant parts of the universe. Already, it has discovered more than 11,000 new asteroids. The data will help scientists track changes in space, study rare cosmic events, and learn more about mysterious things like dark matter and dark energy.

**What's next step** – The observatory will continue collecting data nightly for ten years. Researchers will use this growing collection to make new discoveries about space and improve our understanding of how the universe works.

**One-sentence takeaway** – The Vera C. Rubin Observatory has begun a historic survey that will map the southern sky in unprecedented detail and reveal new cosmic discoveries.
articles.personality_title_fr: L'observatoire Vera C. Rubin lance une enquête décennale pour cartographier l'univers
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – L'observatoire Vera C. Rubin au Chili est une nouvelle installation télescopique conçue pour étudier le ciel austral. Il possède la plus grande caméra jamais construite pour l'astronomie et vise à observer l'univers avec une grande précision.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Après un an de tests, l'observatoire a commencé son Enquête Legacy du Temps et de l'Espace. Pendant les dix prochaines années, il prendra chaque nuit des centaines d'images haute résolution, couvrant presque tout le ciel visible depuis l'hémisphère sud.

**Impact** – Cette enquête créera la carte la plus détaillée du système solaire, de notre galaxie et des parties lointaines de l'univers. Elle a déjà permis de découvrir plus de 11 000 nouveaux astéroïdes. Les données aideront les scientifiques à suivre les changements dans l'espace, étudier des événements cosmiques rares et mieux comprendre des phénomènes mystérieux comme la matière noire et l'énergie sombre.

**Prochaine étape** – L'observatoire continuera de recueillir des données chaque nuit pendant dix ans. Les chercheurs utiliseront cette collection croissante pour faire de nouvelles découvertes sur l'espace et améliorer notre compréhension du fonctionnement de l'univers.

**Résumé en une phrase** – L'observatoire Vera C. Rubin a commencé une enquête historique qui cartographiera le ciel austral avec un niveau de détail sans précédent et révélera de nouvelles découvertes cosmiques.
articles.personality_title_es: El observatorio Vera C. Rubin inicia un estudio de diez años para mapear el universo
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – El observatorio Vera C. Rubin en Chile es una nueva instalación telescópica diseñada para estudiar el cielo del hemisferio sur. Cuenta con la cámara más grande jamás construida para astronomía y busca observar el universo con gran detalle.

**Qué pasó** – Después de un año de pruebas, el observatorio comenzó su Encuesta Legado del Espacio y Tiempo. Durante los próximos diez años, tomará cientos de imágenes de alta resolución cada noche, cubriendo casi todo el cielo visible desde el hemisferio sur.

**Impacto** – Esta encuesta creará el mapa más detallado del sistema solar, nuestra galaxia y partes lejanas del universo. Ya ha descubierto más de 11,000 nuevos asteroides. Los datos ayudarán a los científicos a seguir cambios en el espacio, estudiar eventos cósmicos raros y aprender más sobre fenómenos misteriosos como la materia oscura y la energía oscura.

**Próximo paso** – El observatorio continuará recopilando datos cada noche durante diez años. Los investigadores usarán esta creciente colección para hacer nuevos descubrimientos sobre el espacio y mejorar nuestra comprensión del funcionamiento del universo.

**Resumen en una frase** – El observatorio Vera C. Rubin ha comenzado un estudio histórico que mapeará el cielo del sur con un detalle sin precedentes y revelará nuevos descubrimientos cósmicos.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a vast southern night sky filled with countless stars, swirling interstellar clouds, and distant galaxies, with a large, intricate telescope silhouette gently pointing upward from a serene Chilean landscape, symbolizing the Vera C. Rubin Observatory’s decade-long cosmic survey capturing the dynamic universe.

articles.title: NASA Awards More Moon Base Science, Previews New Opportunities
articles.summary: Editor’s note: This release was updated on June 30, 2026, to clarify the engineering development version for the PROMISE rover. NASA announced Tuesday the selection of three companies to land four new missions on the Moon in late 2028 as part of the agency’s Moon Base Program. Astrobotic, Firefly Aerospace, and Intuitive Machines will deliver […]
articles.content:
Three artist renderings depict commercial lunar landers from Astrobotic, Intuitive Machines, and Firefly on the Moon. NASA announced June 30 the landers will deliver more NASA science investigations and technology demonstrations to the lunar surface for NASA’s Moon Base Program. Credit: Astrobotic/Intuitive Machines/Firefly

Editor’s note: This release was updated on June 30, 2026, to clarify the engineering development version for the PROMISE rover.

NASA announced Tuesday the selection of three companies to land four new missions on the Moon in late 2028 as part of the agency’s Moon Base Program. Astrobotic, Firefly Aerospace, and Intuitive Machines will deliver NASA science payloads to the lunar surface as the agency builds the first outpost on another celestial world.

“These new awards to our commercial partners, totaling nearly $600 million to land more missions on the Moon with science payloads, demonstrate our commitment to accelerating our effort to build a long-term presence on the lunar surface, and give us more opportunity to develop the skills we need to prosper there,” said Lori Glaze, associate administrator for the Human Spaceflight Mission Directorate at NASA Headquarters in Washington.

Astrobotic is awarded $297.9 million total for two deliveries, as well as Firefly Aerospace $144.2 million and Intuitive Machines $148.3 million for one delivery each as part of the agency’s CLPS (Commercial Lunar Payload Services) initiative, a backbone of the Moon Base. Each will use updated versions of already-flown lander designs to enable NASA’s increased mission cadence.

“We’re building a proving ground for Moon Base operations,” said Ryan Stephan, NASA’s Moon Base acting director of cargo landers. “Accelerating our Moon mission ordering cadence and launch opportunities enable us to move quickly to learn, iterate, and improve.”

With 17 lunar surface deliveries across multiple providers, NASA also announced new opportunities for American industry to contribute to the Moon Base. The agency is considering plans to send to the Moon, PROMISE (Polar Rover for Observation, Mapping, and In-Situ Exploration), a hybrid engineering development version of the Mars Perseverance and Curiosity rovers. Agency experts will define potential opportunities for PROMISE to characterize the lunar surface, subsurface, and prospect for resources.

In addition, NASA plans to solicit proposals in the coming months for lunar landers to deliver a power and avionics technology demonstration, another science manifest, and a South Pole optical imager. NASA also will share an open solicitation for Moon Base technology demonstrations and seek a lunar communication and navigation relay constellation to enable improved communication between Moon Base elements and Earth.

The awards announced June 30 will play a critical role in establishing the infrastructure for lunar surface operations. The companies are responsible for initiating and executing procurements, providing an assessment of a similar previous lunar lander, and incorporating lessons learned to improve the overall mission reliability.

Each delivery will carry three NASA payloads to the lunar surface:

Stereo Camera for Lunar Plume Surface Studies (SCALPSS): An array of four cameras that uses a technique called stereo photogrammetry to produce a 3D view of the impact of an engine’s exhaust plume on lunar dust as the lander descends on the Moon’s surface. Collecting data from a variety of engine sizes, propellants, and landing locations, these high-resolution stereo images will aid in creating models to predict lunar dust erosion and ejecta characteristics, playing a vital role as bigger, heavier spacecraft and hardware are delivered to the Moon near each other.

Laser Retroreflector Array (LRA): Reflects laser beams transmitted by Moon orbiters or landing spacecraft to help them determine their orbit position or navigate to the surface. A small cookie-sized device made of eight quartz corner-cube prisms set into a dome-shaped aluminum frame, the array is passive, requiring no power or maintenance. These arrays have flown on previous CLPS landers and international lunar landers and will continue to be used to build a network of permanent location markers on the Moon for future exploration.

Linear Energy Transfer Spectrometer (LETS): Helps to better understand the radiation environment from a variety of lunar transit approaches and at different locations on the lunar surface. Derived from heritage hardware, this radiation monitor uses a tiny, advanced silicon detector to measure the energy carried by incoming space radiation. It will provide information about how strong radiation is and what kind of radiation is hitting the lunar surface, and provides the kind of detailed radiation data NASA needs to design safer missions, protect astronauts, and plan long‑duration exploration.

The agency also is reviewing options for these landers to deliver potential additional payloads to the Moon.

“By flying the same science instruments on multiple landers, we will better understand potential hazards during landing and build out a global network of environmental data and location markers on the Moon,” said Joel Kearns, deputy associate administrator for exploration, Science Mission Directorate, NASA Headquarters. “It’s akin to having weather stations in different locations on Earth. These three payloads are flight-proven and their data is critical to supporting safe human exploration of the lunar surface.”

NASA is advancing development of the Moon Base, a long-term lunar exploration and infrastructure initiative designed to enable sustained human presence and expanded scientific and commercial activity on the lunar surface.

As part of the Golden Age of innovation and exploration, NASA will send astronauts on increasingly difficult missions to explore more of the Moon for scientific discovery, economic benefits, and to build on our foundation for the first crewed missions to Mars.

For more information about NASA’s Moon Base plans, visit:

https://www.nasa.gov/moonbase

-end-

Rachel Kraft / Molly Wasser

Headquarters, Washington

202-358-1600

rachel.h.kraft@nasa.gov / molly.l.wasser@nasa.gov

Ivry Artis / Kenna Pell

Johnson Space Center, Houston

281-483-5111

ivry.w.artis@nasa.gov / kenna.m.pell@nasa.gov
articles.reasoning: The article reports NASA's significant investment and selection of commercial partners to deliver multiple science payloads to the Moon as part of the Moon Base Program, advancing sustainable lunar exploration infrastructure. This has broad significance for space exploration, scientific discovery, and future human presence on the Moon and Mars, with detailed descriptions of the missions, payloads, and technological advancements.
articles.personality_title: NASA selects companies for four new Moon missions to advance Moon Base program
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – NASA is working to build a permanent base on the Moon as part of its long-term plan for space exploration. To do this, it partners with private companies to send science instruments and technology to the lunar surface.

**What happened** – On June 30, 2026, NASA announced it chose three companies—Astrobotic, Firefly Aerospace, and Intuitive Machines—to carry out four missions to the Moon in late 2028. These companies will deliver NASA’s science equipment using updated versions of their lunar landers. The total contracts are worth nearly $600 million, with Astrobotic handling two missions and the others one each.

**Impact** – These missions will help NASA learn more about the Moon’s surface and environment by using instruments like cameras that study dust kicked up by landers, devices that reflect lasers to help spacecraft navigate, and sensors that measure space radiation. Flying the same instruments on different landers will create a network of data points, similar to weather stations on Earth, improving safety and knowledge for future astronauts.

**What’s next step** – NASA plans to open more opportunities soon for companies to work on other Moon Base projects, such as power systems, communications, and new science tools. They are also exploring the idea of sending a special rover called PROMISE to study the Moon’s surface and resources.

**One-sentence takeaway** – NASA’s new contracts with commercial partners will deliver key science tools to the Moon, building the foundation for a lasting human presence there and future Mars missions.
articles.personality_title_fr: La NASA sélectionne des entreprises pour quatre nouvelles missions lunaires afin de faire avancer le programme Moon Base
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – La NASA travaille à la construction d'une base permanente sur la Lune dans le cadre de son plan à long terme pour l'exploration spatiale. Pour cela, elle collabore avec des entreprises privées afin d'envoyer des instruments scientifiques et des technologies sur la surface lunaire.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Le 30 juin 2026, la NASA a annoncé avoir choisi trois entreprises—Astrobotic, Firefly Aerospace et Intuitive Machines—pour réaliser quatre missions sur la Lune à la fin de 2028. Ces entreprises livreront le matériel scientifique de la NASA en utilisant des versions améliorées de leurs atterrisseurs lunaires. Les contrats totalisent près de 600 millions de dollars, Astrobotic réalisant deux missions et les autres une chacune.

**Impact** – Ces missions aideront la NASA à mieux comprendre la surface et l'environnement lunaires grâce à des instruments comme des caméras qui étudient la poussière soulevée par les atterrisseurs, des dispositifs qui réfléchissent des lasers pour aider à la navigation des engins spatiaux, et des capteurs mesurant les radiations spatiales. Faire voler les mêmes instruments sur différents atterrisseurs créera un réseau de données, semblable à des stations météorologiques sur Terre, améliorant la sécurité et les connaissances pour les futurs astronautes.

**Prochaine étape** – La NASA prévoit d'ouvrir prochainement d'autres opportunités pour que des entreprises travaillent sur d'autres projets du Moon Base, tels que des systèmes d'alimentation, des communications et de nouveaux outils scientifiques. Elle étudie aussi l'envoi d'un rover spécial nommé PROMISE pour étudier la surface et les ressources lunaires.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Les nouveaux contrats de la NASA avec des partenaires commerciaux permettront de livrer des outils scientifiques clés sur la Lune, posant les bases d'une présence humaine durable et des futures missions vers Mars.
articles.personality_title_es: La NASA selecciona empresas para cuatro nuevas misiones lunares para avanzar en el programa Moon Base
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La NASA está trabajando para construir una base permanente en la Luna como parte de su plan a largo plazo para la exploración espacial. Para ello, colabora con empresas privadas para enviar instrumentos científicos y tecnología a la superficie lunar.

**Qué pasó** – El 30 de junio de 2026, la NASA anunció que eligió a tres empresas—Astrobotic, Firefly Aerospace e Intuitive Machines—para realizar cuatro misiones en la Luna a finales de 2028. Estas empresas entregarán los equipos científicos de la NASA usando versiones mejoradas de sus módulos de aterrizaje lunar. Los contratos suman casi 600 millones de dólares, con Astrobotic a cargo de dos misiones y las otras dos empresas de una cada una.

**Impacto** – Estas misiones ayudarán a la NASA a aprender más sobre la superficie y el ambiente lunar mediante instrumentos como cámaras que estudian el polvo levantado por los módulos, dispositivos que reflejan láseres para ayudar en la navegación, y sensores que miden la radiación espacial. Usar los mismos instrumentos en diferentes módulos creará una red de datos, similar a estaciones meteorológicas en la Tierra, mejorando la seguridad y el conocimiento para futuros astronautas.

**Próximo paso** – La NASA planea abrir pronto más oportunidades para que las empresas trabajen en otros proyectos del Moon Base, como sistemas de energía, comunicaciones y nuevas herramientas científicas. También está explorando enviar un rover especial llamado PROMISE para estudiar la superficie y los recursos lunares.

**Resumen en una frase** – Los nuevos contratos de la NASA con socios comerciales entregarán herramientas científicas clave a la Luna, construyendo la base para una presencia humana duradera y futuras misiones a Marte.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of three distinct futuristic lunar landers gently touching down on the Moon’s dusty surface near the South Pole, surrounded by softly glowing scientific instruments like a small stereo camera array, a compact retroreflector device, and a tiny radiation detector, all illuminated by Earthlight with a backdrop of a starry black sky and the faint Earth rising on the horizon.

articles.title: Ames Science Stars of the Month July 2026
articles.summary: NASA Ames Science Stars of the Month: July 2026 The NASA Ames Science Directorate recognizes the outstanding contributions of (pictured left to right) Sungshin Choi, Yi-Chun Chen, Emma Yates, Eduardo Bendek. Their commitment to the NASA mission represents the entrepreneurial spirit, technical expertise, and collaborative disposition needed to explore this world and beyond. Space Biosciences […]
articles.content:
NASA Ames Science Stars of the Month: July 2026

Pictured left to right: Sungshin Choi, Yi-Chun Chen, Emma Yates, Eduardo Bendek

The NASA Ames Science Directorate recognizes the outstanding contributions of (pictured left to right) Sungshin Choi, Yi-Chun Chen, Emma Yates, Eduardo Bendek. Their commitment to the NASA mission represents the entrepreneurial spirit, technical expertise, and collaborative disposition needed to explore this world and beyond.

Space Biosciences Star: Sungshin Choi

Sungshin Choi is a Project Scientist with Amentum in the Space Biosciences Division. Sungshin is recognized for her enduring support of many space biology flight investigations past, present and future, including CBIOMES, ODYSSEY, and Space Algae II more recently. She is a tireless advocate for high-quality science and the principal investigators whom she represents.

Space Biosciences Star: Yi-Chun Chen

Yi-Chun Chen is a Project Scientist with Amentum in the Space Biosciences Division. Yi-Chun is recognized for her exemplary support of multiple space biology activities including the MeF1, GEARS, and ELISA MABL (Enzyme-Linked Immunosorbent Assay – Microgravity Associated Bone Loss) flight investigations. She is a dedicated and determined problem-solver that enables her teams to achieve success.

Emma Yates

Earth Science Star: Emma Yates

Emma Yates is a research scientist with the Bay Area Environmental Research Institute in the Earth Science Division. She has been instrumental in advancing NASA’s Ozone Where We Live (OWWL) project by leading community engagement, citizen-science partnerships, and field deployments across California. Her efforts are expanding access to NASA science while building innovative community-based air quality monitoring networks that support Earth science research and public engagement.

Space Science Star: Eduardo Bendek

Eduardo Bendek is an optical scientist with the SETI Institute in the Astrophysics Branch in the Space Science and Astrobiology Division. In support of the Ames Coronagraph Testbed (ACT), Eduardo developed several options for ACT first light experiments, reviewed them with various stakeholders, and delivered a comprehensive presentation to project management for how to proceed. Eduardo’s excellent support of the ACT project is critical to its success as Ames develops this near-infrared testbed for the Habitable Worlds Observatory.
articles.reasoning: The article highlights the recognition of NASA Ames scientists for their significant contributions to space biosciences, earth science, and space science projects. These contributions support important NASA missions and research with broad implications for space exploration and environmental monitoring, representing inspiring scientific progress and collaboration.
articles.personality_title: NASA Ames honors July 2026 science stars advancing space and Earth research
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – NASA Ames Research Center supports many important science projects that help us understand space, Earth’s environment, and how to explore beyond our planet.

**What happened** – In July 2026, NASA Ames recognized four scientists for their outstanding work: Sungshin Choi and Yi-Chun Chen in space biosciences, Emma Yates in Earth science, and Eduardo Bendek in space science. Each has played a key role in projects like space biology experiments, community air quality studies, and developing instruments for exploring other worlds.

**Impact** – These scientists helped improve important NASA missions by supporting experiments on how life reacts to space conditions, creating partnerships with communities to monitor air pollution, and designing advanced tools for discovering habitable planets. Their work shows how teamwork and expertise can push science forward in different areas.

**What's next step** – The projects they support will continue growing, with future space biology experiments, more community science involvement in air quality, and further development of instruments for the Habitable Worlds Observatory. Their efforts lay the groundwork for new discoveries about space and Earth.

**One-sentence takeaway** – NASA Ames celebrated four scientists whose dedication is advancing key research in space biology, Earth monitoring, and tools to explore distant planets.
articles.personality_title_fr: NASA Ames honore les étoiles scientifiques de juillet 2026 qui font progresser la recherche spatiale et terrestre
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Le centre de recherche NASA Ames soutient de nombreux projets scientifiques importants qui nous aident à comprendre l’espace, l’environnement terrestre et comment explorer au-delà de notre planète.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – En juillet 2026, NASA Ames a reconnu quatre scientifiques pour leur travail remarquable : Sungshin Choi et Yi-Chun Chen en biosciences spatiales, Emma Yates en sciences de la Terre, et Eduardo Bendek en sciences spatiales. Chacun a joué un rôle clé dans des projets tels que des expériences de biologie spatiale, des études communautaires sur la qualité de l’air, et le développement d’instruments pour explorer d’autres mondes.

**Impact** – Ces scientifiques ont aidé à améliorer des missions importantes de la NASA en soutenant des expériences sur la réaction de la vie aux conditions spatiales, en créant des partenariats avec les communautés pour surveiller la pollution de l’air, et en concevant des outils avancés pour découvrir des planètes habitables. Leur travail montre comment le travail d’équipe et l’expertise peuvent faire avancer la science dans différents domaines.

**Prochaines étapes** – Les projets qu’ils soutiennent continueront de se développer, avec de futures expériences en biologie spatiale, une implication communautaire accrue dans la surveillance de la qualité de l’air, et un développement supplémentaire des instruments pour l’Observatoire des mondes habitables. Leurs efforts préparent le terrain pour de nouvelles découvertes sur l’espace et la Terre.

**Résumé en une phrase** – NASA Ames a célébré quatre scientifiques dont le dévouement fait progresser la recherche clé en biologie spatiale, surveillance terrestre et outils pour explorer des planètes lointaines.
articles.personality_title_es: NASA Ames reconoce a las estrellas científicas de julio 2026 que avanzan en la investigación espacial y terrestre
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – El Centro de Investigación NASA Ames apoya muchos proyectos científicos importantes que nos ayudan a entender el espacio, el medio ambiente de la Tierra y cómo explorar más allá de nuestro planeta.

**Qué pasó** – En julio de 2026, NASA Ames reconoció a cuatro científicos por su trabajo sobresaliente: Sungshin Choi y Yi-Chun Chen en bio-ciencias espaciales, Emma Yates en ciencias de la Tierra, y Eduardo Bendek en ciencias espaciales. Cada uno ha desempeñado un papel clave en proyectos como experimentos de biología espacial, estudios comunitarios sobre la calidad del aire y el desarrollo de instrumentos para explorar otros mundos.

**Impacto** – Estos científicos ayudaron a mejorar misiones importantes de la NASA apoyando experimentos sobre cómo la vida reacciona a las condiciones del espacio, creando asociaciones con comunidades para monitorear la contaminación del aire y diseñando herramientas avanzadas para descubrir planetas habitables. Su trabajo muestra cómo el trabajo en equipo y la experiencia pueden impulsar la ciencia en diferentes áreas.

**Próximo paso** – Los proyectos que apoyan seguirán creciendo, con futuros experimentos de biología espacial, mayor participación comunitaria en el monitoreo de la calidad del aire y desarrollo continuo de instrumentos para el Observatorio Mundos Habitables. Sus esfuerzos preparan el camino para nuevos descubrimientos sobre el espacio y la Tierra.

**Conclusión en una frase** – NASA Ames celebró a cuatro científicos cuyo compromiso está impulsando investigaciones clave en biología espacial, monitoreo terrestre y herramientas para explorar planetas distantes.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of four glowing orbs floating above a stylized landscape that blends a vibrant microscopic view of algae and cells, a network of interconnected sensors over a California map, and a sleek telescope with light beams projecting into a starry cosmos, all rendered in soft, natural hues to symbolize collaboration and scientific exploration.
